---
title: "Visualization with seaborn"
description: "Create statistical plots with seaborn."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [data-science]
order: 610
colab_notebook: "notebooks/lessons/data-science/visualization-with-seaborn.ipynb"
---
::: {.lesson-meta}
**Level:** Beginner to intermediate

**Estimated time:** 25–40 minutes

**You will learn:** Create statistical plots with seaborn.

**Practice in:** Jupyter or Google Colab
:::

{{< include ../_includes/colab-link.qmd >}}

<!-- fcpython-lab: start -->
## Questions

- What problem does **Visualization with seaborn** help us solve in a small Python program?
- What should we predict before running the example?
- What value, output, or error should we inspect after changing one line?

## Objectives

- Run a complete example for **visualization with seaborn** in Colab.
- Explain the example line by line using plain language.
- Change one part of the code and predict the result before running it.
- Recognize one common mistake and use the error message as evidence.

## Hands-on episode: Visualization with seaborn

Seaborn maps DataFrame columns to visual features such as x position, y position, color, and facets. The best plot type depends on the question and variable types.

We will learn this by running code, not by memorizing a definition first.
Open the Colab notebook from the button above, find this section, and run each
cell in order. Keep a small note beside the notebook with three columns:
**prediction**, **actual result**, and **what changed**.

### Example 1.1

Predict what each point represents in the scatter plot.

```python
import seaborn as sns
import pandas as pd

data = pd.DataFrame({"hours": [1, 2, 3], "score": [60, 75, 85]})
sns.scatterplot(data=data, x="hours", y="score")
```

Run the cell once without editing it. If the result is different from your
prediction, leave the prediction visible and write one sentence about the
difference. That sentence is more useful than a perfect first guess.

### Explain Example 1.1

- Each row becomes one point.
- The `hours` column controls horizontal position.
- The `score` column controls vertical position.
- The plot helps you inspect whether more hours seem associated with higher scores.

Now explain the example out loud or in a Markdown cell. Use short sentences:
"this line creates...", "this name stores...", "this output appears because...".
If you cannot explain a line yet, run only the lines above it and inspect the
values that exist at that moment.

### Challenge 1.1

::: {.callout-note title="Challenge"}
Change the plot to a line plot and ask whether that honestly represents the data. Plot type should match the story: relationships, distributions, comparisons, or change over order.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show a safe way to approach the challenge</summary>

1. Copy Example 1.1 into a new Colab cell.
2. Change exactly one value, name, condition, or line.
3. Write the expected output before running the cell.
4. Run the cell and compare the actual result with your prediction.
5. If the result surprises you, undo the change and try a smaller one.

Suggested first move: Change the plot to a line plot and ask whether that honestly represents the data.

</details>

### Debugging checkpoint 1.1

::: {.callout-warning title="Debugging checkpoint"}
Avoid unlabeled or overplotted charts. If points overlap heavily, consider transparency, jitter, a different plot type, or aggregation. Always write a caption that states the main finding and a limitation.
:::

Do not debug by rewriting the whole example. Read the error type or surprising
output, inspect the closest value with `print(...)` or `type(...)`, then change
one thing. This is the same routine you will use in larger projects.

### Apply it

Make one scatter plot, one bar plot, or one histogram from a small DataFrame. Add labels and write a caption explaining the visual evidence.

Finish by adding a Markdown cell that answers: **What did this example teach
me that I can reuse in a project?**

## Key points

- Learn the concept by running a complete, small example first.
- Predict before execution so your thinking becomes visible.
- Change one thing at a time so cause and effect stay clear.
- Treat errors as clues about the exact line or value Python could not handle.
<!-- fcpython-lab: end -->


## Why this matters

Create statistical plots with seaborn.

Beginners often try to learn Python as a list of commands. This lesson teaches one idea
at a time: what problem it solves, how to recognize it in code, how to practice it, and
how to debug a common mistake. Treat the code examples as small experiments, not as text
to memorize.

::: {.callout-note title="Guiding questions"}
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- What problem does **Visualization with seaborn** help me solve?
- What words or symbols should I recognize in a small example?
- What should I check first when the example does not behave as expected?
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Learning objectives"}
You will practice how to:

- explain the main idea in everyday language;
- read a short example line by line;
- predict the result before running the code;
- make one safe variation of the example;
- debug one common beginner mistake.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
seaborn is like graph paper with helpful templates for common data stories.
:::

## Vocabulary

- **seaborn** — a key term for this lesson; after the example, write a one-sentence definition in your own words.
- **scatterplot** — a key term for this lesson; after the example, write a one-sentence definition in your own words.
- **hue** — a key term for this lesson; after the example, write a one-sentence definition in your own words.
- **DataFrame** — a key term for this lesson; after the example, write a one-sentence definition in your own words.
- **statistical plot** — a key term for this lesson; after the example, write a one-sentence definition in your own words.

## What this means

seaborn builds statistical visualizations on top of Matplotlib and works well with
DataFrames.

A useful explanation has three parts:

1. **Name the thing.** Say what concept you are using.
2. **Name the input.** Identify the values, files, objects, or settings involved.
3. **Name the result.** Explain what changes, what is returned, or what is printed.

## Example 1
Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import seaborn as sns

tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")
sns.scatterplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip", hue="time")
```

## Step-by-step explanation

1. `import seaborn as sns` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
2. `tips = sns.load_dataset("tips")` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
3. `sns.scatterplot(data=tips, x="total_bill", y="tip", hue="time")` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

## Worked example: read, predict, modify

Use this routine with every example in the course:

| Step | What to do | Why it helps |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Read | Point to each name, value, and operator. | Slows the code down enough to understand it. |
| Predict | Write what you think will happen. | Creates a testable prediction. |
| Run | Execute the smallest complete example. | Lets Python give evidence. |
| Explain | Say what happened in plain language. | Converts recognition into understanding. |
| Modify | Change one small thing and run again. | Shows which part caused which result. |

## Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy Example 1 into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

## Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
A visualization should answer a question. Add titles, labels, and context before
sharing.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

## Checkpoint quiz

::: {.quiz-box}
Answer these questions before moving on. The quiz runs with Quarto OJS in the
browser, so it does not need a Python kernel during website rendering.
:::

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-science-visualization-with-seaborn",
  "title": "Visualization with seaborn checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Visualization with seaborn?",
      "options": [
        "seaborn builds statistical visualizations on top of Matplotlib and works well with DataFrames.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: seaborn builds statistical visualizations on top of Matplotlib and works well with DataFrames."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: seaborn is like graph paper with helpful templates for common data stories."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after Example 1 works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at https://colab.new, copy Example 1, and run three small
variations. You can also use a local Jupyter notebook or VS Code.
Keep one cell for the original example, one cell for your prediction, and one
cell for your modified version.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Ask learners to predict before execution; do not skip this step.
- Invite one learner to explain the analogy and another to explain the code.
- When an error appears, model calm traceback reading instead of immediately fixing it.
- If time is short, keep Example 1 and quiz; move the challenge to homework.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.callout-tip title="Key points"}
- seaborn builds statistical visualizations on top of Matplotlib and works well with DataFrames.
- Small examples are more useful than large copied programs when a concept is new.
- Prediction, execution, explanation, and one small modification form the core practice loop.
- Debugging starts by reading clues and changing one thing at a time.
:::

## References

- seaborn documentation: https://seaborn.pydata.org/
- pandas documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/
- Matplotlib documentation: https://matplotlib.org/stable/
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
